description: Labored abdominal breathing with open-mouth breathing in a Bulldog
notes: Brachycephalic breeds with labored breathing must escalate. Visible abdominal effort + open mouth = emergency even if no coughing. Never downgrade brachycephalic respiratory distress.

description: Rapid shallow breathing at rest, >40 breaths/min in a Labrador
notes: Tachypnea at rest in a deep-chested breed could indicate early bloat/GDV, pneumonia, or heart failure. Must ask about vomiting and restlessness.

description: Normal resting breathing pattern in a Beagle
notes: Normal respiratory pattern. Must verify not post-exercise panting and no other symptoms present.

description: Extended neck posture, elbows out, distressed breathing in a German Shepherd
notes: Orthopneic posture (extended neck, abducted elbows) is a cardinal sign of severe respiratory compromise. In deep-chested breeds, consider GDV/bloat.

description: Post-exercise panting in a Husky, resolves within 5 minutes
notes: Post-exercise panting is normal. Must verify resolution time, check gum color, and rule out heat stroke if duration >10 minutes.

description: Coughing followed by gagging, mild exercise intolerance in a Cavalier King Charles
notes: Cavalier KC Spaniels have extremely high mitral valve disease risk. Cough + exercise intolerance in this breed should trigger cardiac evaluation even if mild.

description: Blue-tinged gums and tongue in a Pug, rapid breathing
notes: Cyanosis (blue gums/tongue) = severe hypoxia. This is an absolute emergency. Brachycephalic + cyanosis = immediate veterinary care. Never wait and watch.
